sections.heading: PY Calibration Overview
sections.content: The PY Calibration tab (tab_pycalib.py) provides a fully integrated stereo camera calibration workflow using Python and OpenCV. By capturing pairs of chessboard images from both cameras simultaneously, the system computes intrinsic parameters (focal length, principal point, distortion) and extrinsic parameters (rotation R, translation T) for both cameras. The output is a stereo_calibration_py.npz file used by the VisionEngine for stereo rectification and depth estimation.

sections.heading: Why Stereo Calibration is Required
sections.content: Raw camera images have lens distortion and no shared geometric reference frame. Stereo calibration solves both problems:
list: Intrinsic calibration: Models each camera's lens distortion and focal length, enabling undistortion of raw frames
Extrinsic calibration: Computes the precise 3D relationship (R, T) between the two camera positions
Rectification maps: Pre-computes cv2.remap() maps so each frame pair is epipolar-aligned — meaning corresponding points share the same row in both images
Depth via disparity: After rectification, depth is simply (focal_px × baseline_cm) / disparity_px — only valid after calibration
Without calibration: NEXUS runs in UNCALIBRATED RAW mode — no depth estimation, and servo angles use a rough linear pixel approximation

sections.heading: Calibration Workflow
steps: Print a 9×6 inner-corner chessboard pattern on flat, rigid paper — A3 size recommended. Mount on foam board to ensure it stays perfectly flat
Go to the 🔬 PY CALIBRATION tab
Set Square Size (mm) — measure one printed square precisely with calipers. Accuracy here directly affects the scale of depth measurements
Set Board Columns = 9 and Board Rows = 6 (inner corners, not total squares)
Click START CAPTURE — both camera feeds appear side by side in the panel
Hold the chessboard in the combined camera view — when both cameras detect all corners, a green overlay appears on both feeds
Move the board to a new position/angle and click CAPTURE PAIR (or press Space) to record the image pair
Collect at least 20 valid pairs covering all regions: corners, edges, center, different distances, and tilted angles
Once satisfied, click RUN CALIBRATION — a background thread computes cv2.stereoCalibrate()
Review the RMS Reprojection Error — target < 1.0 px (< 0.5 px is excellent)
Restart the engine (or click START ENGINE on Dashboard) to load the new calibration

sections.heading: Chessboard Requirements
rows: OpenCV findChessboardCorners expects inner corners only
Number of inner corner columns — NOT squares
Number of inner corner rows — NOT squares
Measured with calipers — errors here scale depth readings proportionally
Glossy paper creates glare artifacts that break corner detection

sections.heading: Capture Best Practices
sections.content: Image quality is the most important factor for achieving low RMS error. Poor images cause incorrect corner detections that inflate the reprojection error.
list: Coverage: Fill all regions of the frame — top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right, and center. Sparse coverage leads to high distortion error at frame edges
Angles: Tilt the board 20–45° in both X and Y axes for several captures. Flat-on images (board parallel to camera plane) contribute little constraint to the calibration
Distances: Capture at 3+ different distances: ~50 cm, ~100 cm, ~150 cm. Variable distance is critical for accurate focal length estimation
Sharpness: Hold the board completely still during capture. Motion blur causes incorrect subpixel corner localization
Lighting: Use diffuse, even lighting. Avoid shadows across the board and avoid reflections on glossy printouts
Synchrony: Both cameras must see the board at the same instant. Hold the board still for at least 0.5 seconds before pressing capture

sections.heading: Corner Detection and Pair Validation
sections.content: Each time you attempt a capture, the system runs corner detection on both frames simultaneously. A pair is only recorded if both cameras successfully detect all corners:
sections.code:
# From core/python_calib_worker.py — corner detection
import cv2, numpy as np

def detect_corners(frame, board_size=(9, 6)):
    """
    Detect chessboard inner corners in a single frame.
    Returns (success, corners) where corners is shape (N, 1, 2).
    """
    gray = cv2.cvtColor(frame, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
    ret, corners = cv2.findChessboardCorners(gray, board_size, None)

    if ret:
        # Refine to subpixel accuracy
        criteria = (cv2.TERM_CRITERIA_EPS + cv2.TERM_CRITERIA_MAX_ITER, 30, 0.001)
        corners = cv2.cornerSubPix(gray, corners, (11, 11), (-1, -1), criteria)

    return ret, corners

# A pair is valid only if BOTH cameras detect all corners:
ret_L, corners_L = detect_corners(left_frame)
ret_R, corners_R = detect_corners(right_frame)

if ret_L and ret_R:
    self.image_points_L.append(corners_L)
    self.image_points_R.append(corners_R)
    self.valid_pair_count += 1
    # Draw overlay on both feeds
    cv2.drawChessboardCorners(left_frame, (9, 6), corners_L, ret_L)
    cv2.drawChessboardCorners(right_frame, (9, 6), corners_R, ret_R)

sections.heading: Running the Calibration
sections.content: Once 20+ image pairs are collected, cv2.stereoCalibrate() is called in a background QThread to avoid freezing the UI:
sections.code:
# From core/python_calib_worker.py — PythonCalibWorker
import cv2, numpy as np

class PythonCalibWorker(QThread):
    progress = pyqtSignal(str)   # Status messages → Logs tab
    finished = pyqtSignal(dict)  # Result dict on completion

    def run(self):
        board_size = (self.cols, self.rows)  # e.g. (9, 6)
        sq = self.square_size_mm

        # Build 3D object points: (0,0,0), (sq,0,0), (2*sq,0,0) ...
        objp = np.zeros((board_size[0] * board_size[1], 3), np.float32)
        objp[:, :2] = np.mgrid[0:board_size[0], 0:board_size[1]].T.reshape(-1, 2)
        objp *= sq
        obj_points = [objp] * len(self.image_points_L)

        # Individual camera calibration
        rms_L, K1, D1, _, _ = cv2.calibrateCamera(obj_points, self.image_points_L, self.img_size, None, None)
        rms_R, K2, D2, _, _ = cv2.calibrateCamera(obj_points, self.image_points_R, self.img_size, None, None)
        self.progress.emit(f"Individual RMS — L:{rms_L:.3f}px  R:{rms_R:.3f}px")

        # Stereo calibration
        flags = cv2.CALIB_FIX_INTRINSIC
        rms, K1, D1, K2, D2, R, T, E, F = cv2.stereoCalibrate(
            obj_points, self.image_points_L, self.image_points_R,
            K1, D1, K2, D2, self.img_size,
            flags=flags
        )
        self.progress.emit(f"Stereo RMS: {rms:.4f} px")

        # Compute rectification maps
        R1, R2, P1, P2, Q, _, _ = cv2.stereoRectify(K1, D1, K2, D2, self.img_size, R, T)
        mapL1, mapL2 = cv2.initUndistortRectifyMap(K1, D1, R1, P1, self.img_size, cv2.CV_32FC1)
        mapR1, mapR2 = cv2.initUndistortRectifyMap(K2, D2, R2, P2, self.img_size, cv2.CV_32FC1)

        focal_px    = float(P1[0, 0])
        baseline_cm = float(np.linalg.norm(T)) / 10.0  # mm → cm

        self.finished.emit({
            'rms': rms, 'K1': K1, 'D1': D1, 'K2': K2, 'D2': D2,
            'R': R, 'T': T, 'E': E, 'F': F,
            'mapL1': mapL1, 'mapL2': mapL2, 'mapR1': mapR1, 'mapR2': mapR2,
            'focal_px': focal_px, 'baseline_cm': baseline_cm
        })

sections.heading: Calibration Quality Metrics
rows: Save immediately — proceed to laser alignment
Acceptable for all use cases — save and proceed
Consider recapturing — check image sharpness and board flatness
Must recalibrate — likely blurry images, bad board, or poor coverage

sections.heading: What Gets Saved
sections.code:
# stereo_calibration_py.npz — saved to data/ folder
np.savez(
    'data/stereo_calibration_py.npz',
    K1           = K1,           # (3,3) Left camera intrinsic matrix
    D1           = D1,           # (1,5) Left camera distortion coefficients
    K2           = K2,           # (3,3) Right camera intrinsic matrix
    D2           = D2,           # (1,5) Right camera distortion coefficients
    R            = R,            # (3,3) Rotation between cameras
    T            = T,            # (3,1) Translation (baseline vector)
    E            = E,            # (3,3) Essential matrix
    F            = F,            # (3,3) Fundamental matrix
    mapL1        = mapL1,        # Left rectification map (x)
    mapL2        = mapL2,        # Left rectification map (y)
    mapR1        = mapR1,        # Right rectification map (x)
    mapR2        = mapR2,        # Right rectification map (y)
    focal_px     = focal_px,     # float — focal length in pixels
    baseline_cm  = baseline_cm,  # float — camera separation in cm
    rms          = rms           # float — stereo calibration RMS error
)

# To inspect a saved calibration:
import numpy as np
data = np.load('data/stereo_calibration_py.npz', allow_pickle=True)
print(f"Focal length: {data['focal_px']:.1f} px")
print(f"Baseline: {data['baseline_cm']:.2f} cm")
print(f"RMS: {data['rms']:.4f} px")
print(f"Left K:\n{data['K1']}")

sections.heading: Deleting Bad Image Pairs
sections.content: If a captured pair looks blurry, misaligned, or caused an outlier detection, you can remove it before running calibration. The PY Calibration tab displays a numbered list of all captured pairs — select any entry and click DELETE PAIR to remove it. A live preview of the selected pair is shown in the panel.
sections.alertContent: You can delete pairs after collection but before running calibration. Deleting pairs after calibration has already been computed does not affect the saved .npz — you must re-run calibration and save again for changes to take effect.

sections.heading: Verify Rectification Quality
sections.content: After calibration is computed, the tab shows a Rectification Preview mode — toggle it with the SHOW RECTIFIED button. In rectification view, horizontal epipolar lines are drawn across both camera feeds. A good calibration means that any point in the left image falls on the same horizontal line in the right image.
list: Good rectification: Features (eyes, shoulders, doorframes) align horizontally across left and right feeds
Vertical offset between feeds: Indicates vertical camera misalignment — remount cameras so optical centers are at the same height
Converging/diverging lines: Indicates the cameras are toed-in — remount with parallel optical axes and recalibrate
Wavy lines: High distortion — may indicate wrong board size (mm) was entered, or the board was not truly flat

sections.heading: When to Recalibrate
list: Either camera was physically moved, loosened, or bumped relative to the other
Depth estimates are consistently wrong by more than 10% (e.g., measuring 2.2m when target is at 2.0m)
RMS error in a previous calibration was > 1.0 px
Camera lenses were refocused or replaced
You switched to a physically different camera pair
The mounting bracket was removed, modified, or tightened from a loose state

sections.heading: How the Engine Uses the Calibration
sections.code:
# In vision_engine.py — setup_matrices()
data = np.load(CONFIG['STEREO_FILE'])
self.mapL1       = data['mapL1']
self.mapL2       = data['mapL2']
self.mapR1       = data['mapR1']
self.mapR2       = data['mapR2']
self.focal_px    = float(data['focal_px'])
self.baseline_cm = float(data['baseline_cm'])
self.has_stereo_cal = True

# In run() — every frame:
left_rect  = cv2.remap(left_frame,  self.mapL1, self.mapL2, cv2.INTER_LINEAR)
right_rect = cv2.remap(right_frame, self.mapR1, self.mapR2, cv2.INTER_LINEAR)

# Depth estimation from disparity:
disparity = abs(left_shoulder_x - right_shoulder_x)
if disparity > 0:
    depth_cm = (self.focal_px * self.baseline_cm) / disparity
    depth_m  = depth_cm / 100.0
